next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

By half three the frost still clung to the east window, and Granger wanted the examination done before the light failed.

For three days she'd been rebuilding the diagnostic array, checking her notes against the archive texts, talking herself toward a calibration she didn't entirely trust. Surface readings told them how the Mark was misfiring now: damaged channels, cyclical spikes, cursed magic trying to run through routes that no longer connected to anything. What she wanted today was older than that. If the original casting still sat intact under the later damage, it might be holding more than structure.

"Residue from the bind itself," she'd said over tea that morning, eyes on her notes instead of on him. "Not just the spell's shape. If the first layer kept any lived trace, pressure, sequence, sensory carryover, I need to know before I keep putting my hands into it blind."

He'd said fine. He'd been saying fine for weeks.

They used the same small study off the library corridor, its desk already lit by sconces because a December afternoon at the Manor went dark early.

Across from her, Draco rolled his left sleeve to the elbow.

The first weeks had made that motion feel like exposure. Now it was routine: button, fold, forearm on the desk. Routine did not make the skull and serpent less ugly. It only made the sight of it survivable.

Cool fingers bracketed his wrist. Her right palm settled over the Mark.

"I'm going past the surface," she said. "The first layers should feel ordinary. If the older magic wakes up, it may not."

"And you'll know what that feels...
open_hooks_to_carry: Narcissa and Hermione alone — careful, layered, two intelligent women circling. Narcissa's history with the house rendered as exhausted intimacy, not mystique
Narcissa's warning: the house is not malicious but not neutral
Research progress: magical decay tied to war damage to ancient anchoring infrastructure; the Malfoys helped build it

payoffs_to_land.description: Narcissa gives partial information about the Manor's bonding history — enough to seed doubt without naming the mechanism

prev_tail_excerpt:
...an a minute. Work took them again.

A Malfoy ancestor whose name he didn't recognize had described the house as *attendant*, a word that came closer to listening than watching. The passage should have clarified matters. It did not. Six hundred years earlier, someone in his line had already accepted that the Manor tracked its inhabitants and altered conditions accordingly. Precedent existed. Documentation existed. None of it made the present less unnerving.

Across from him, Granger had tucked both feet under herself and forgotten they belonged to a body at all. When she reached for a second scroll, her shoulder brushed the stone, then stayed there. Once, shifting position, she nudged his ankle with her socked foot and kept reading as if she had not noticed. Perhaps she had not. He had.

The archive held. Heat worked slowly up through the floor. No daylight marked the passage of time in that room, but eventually the lamp became necessary and then insufficient on its own until the house answered with a sourceless glow in the seams of the ceiling.

They kept reading.

By full dark, copies of anchoring diagrams covered the mapping board. Half a page of correlations linked war-damaged sites to older reinforcement nodes. On the floor between them sat a teapot gone cold and a pile of notes dense enough to matter. She passed him a scroll when she stalled at a family cipher. He translated it, slid her the next folio, and marked the shelf number before her quill lifted from the page. The house had forced a condition.

That did not make him trust the house.

It did not make him leave.
secondary_character_beats: Narcissa as a full person: her social position (neither pardoned nor convicted), her awareness that the house treated her marriage differently, her protective instinct toward Draco that she expresses through strategic partial truth

setups_to_plant.description: What Narcissa holds back here — the Manor cannot fabricate connection, only amplify it — is the specific knowledge that will reframe the rupture in Ch18

state_deltas_required: Narcissa carries knowledge about the Manor's bonding history that she reveals obliquely — enough to unsettle, not enough to resolve
The Manor has agenda and precedent. Narcissa's lived experience adds a generational layer to the house's behavior. The magical decay research points to deep structural damage
